fullDescription.text: The application parses user-supplied XML without disabling external entity resolution, allowing attackers to read arbitrary files or trigger SSRF via DOCTYPE/ENTITY declarations.
XXE with external entity expansion allows the server to make arbitrary outbound HTTP/FTP requests to attacker-controlled or internal hosts, constituting SSRF.

shortDescription.text: XML bomb (billion laughs) denial of service

fullDescription.text: The endpoint reads the full request body as XML with no size limit, content-type validation, or entity expansion depth restriction. An attacker can submit a recursive entity expansion payload (billion laughs) to exhaust server memory and CPU.

shortDescription.text: Exception details leaked to client in error response

fullDescription.text: XML parsing exception details are returned directly to the client. This can reveal internal file paths, library versions, and parser configuration details that aid further attacks.

message.text: ET.fromstring() is called with a default ET.XMLParser() that does not disable external entity resolution. An attacker can supply a DOCTYPE with an ENTITY referencing a local file (e.g. file:///etc/passwd) or a remote URL. Python's xml.etree.ElementTree backed by expat blocks some XXE by default, but the explicit XMLParser without defensive configuration and the absence of defusedxml makes this an insecure pattern that is exploitable if the backend is ever swapped to lxml.

If the underlying XML parser resolves external entities (e.g. via a SYSTEM identifier pointing to an http:// URL), the server will make outbound requests to attacker-controlled hosts. This constitutes SSRF and can be used to probe internal services or cloud metadata endpoints (e.g. http://169.254.169.254/).

The endpoint reads request.data without any size limit or content-type validation, then passes it directly to the XML parser. An attacker can send a billion laughs payload (recursive entity expansion) or an extremely large XML document to exhaust server memory and CPU, causing denial of service. Use defusedxml which limits entity expansion depth, or enforce a request body size limit.

The except clause returns str(e) directly in the JSON error response. XML parsing errors can reveal internal file paths, library version strings, and parser configuration details to an attacker, aiding reconnaissance for further exploitation.
